FULL CONTENT:
# A hybrid chemical-biological approach can upcycle mixed plastic waste with reduced cost and carbon footprint

Derived from renewable feedstocks, such as biomass, polylactic acid (PLA) is considered a more environmentally friendly plastic than conventional petroleum-based polyethylene terephthalate (PET). However, PLA must still be recycled, and its growing popularity and mixture with PET plastics at the disposal stage poses a cross-contamination threat in existing recycling facilities and results in low-value and low-quality recycled products. Hybrid upcycling has been proposed as a promising sustainable solution for mixed plastic waste, but its techno-economic and life cycle environmental performance remain understudied. We propose a hybrid upcycling approach using a biocompatible ionic liquid (IL) to first chemically depolymerize plastics and then convert the depolymerized stream via biological upgrading with no extra separation. Here we show that over 95% of mixed PET/PLA was depolymerized into the respective monomers, which then served as the sole carbon source for the growth of Pseudomonas putida, enabling the conversion of the depolymerized plastics into biodegradable polyhydroxyalkanoates (PHAs). In comparison to conventional commercial PHAs, the estimated optimal production cost and carbon footprint are reduced by 62% and 29%, respectively.

# Sustainable waste management through polymer upcycling

Polymers are polluting our planet. Upcycling offers an alternative, sustainable path to convert them into high performance materials, fuels and useful chemicals involving chemical, biological, mechanical or hybrid strategies. With this cross-journal Collection, the editors at *Communications Sustainability*, *Communications Chemistry*, *Nature Communications*, *npj Materials Sustainability*, and *Scientific Reports* invite manuscripts that highlight innovative research that transforms waste into new materials or products in smart, energy efficient, scalable and eco-friendly ways through polymer upcycling for a cleaner and more sustainable world.

*Communications Sustainability*, *Communications Chemistry*, *Nature Communications*, and *npj Materials Sustainability* will consider original Articles, Reviews and Perspectives. *Scientific Reports* will consider original Articles.

![A mountain of plastic goods at a recycling plant.](https://media.springernature.com/w580h326/nature-cms/uploads/collections/efbihhfahe-e50c7d37d58f6b3be449a24408f1453a.jpg)
![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41598-025-05577-x/MediaObjects/41598_2025_5577_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [Impact of recycling on polymer binder integrity in metal injection molding](/articles/s41598-025-05577-x)

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41467-025-59540-5/MediaObjects/41467_2025_59540_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [Facile visible-light upcycling of diverse waste plastics using a single organocatalyst with minimal loadings](/articles/s41467-025-59540-5)

The escalating plastic waste crisis stems from limitations in conventional recycling methods, which are energy intensive and produce lower-quality materials, leaving a substantial portion unrecycled. Here, the authors report an organo-photocatalytic upcycling method employing an easily accessible phenothiazine derivative to selectively deconstruct a wide array of commodity polymers.

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41598-025-94925-y/MediaObjects/41598_2025_94925_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [Thermoanalytical approach to assess riverine PET litter and its recycling potential](/articles/s41598-025-94925-y)

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41467-025-57821-7/MediaObjects/41467_2025_57821_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [Upcycling waste commodity polymers into high-performance polyarylate materials with direct utilization of capping agent impurities](/articles/s41467-025-57821-7)

Recycling of commodity plastics remains challenging due to the presence of additives and mixed waste streams. Here the authors develop a strategy for co-upcycling polycarbonate, and polyethylene terephthalate, two types of waste commodity polymers, into polyarylate, a high-performance transparent engineering plastic.

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs44296-024-00045-5/MediaObjects/44296_2024_45_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [Crucial role of pre-treatment in plastic photoreforming for precision upcycling](/articles/s44296-024-00045-5)

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41598-024-73775-0/MediaObjects/41598_2024_73775_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [The emission and physicochemical properties of airborne microplastics and nanoplastics generated during the mechanical recycling of plastic via shredding](/articles/s41598-024-73775-0)

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs42004-023-00949-8/MediaObjects/42004_2023_949_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [A facile alternative strategy of upcycling mixed plastic waste into vitrimers](/articles/s42004-023-00949-8)

Chemical depolymerization is a promising approach to recycle plastic waste, but complete depolymerization is energy-intense. Here, the authors show upcycling of mixed plastic waste to highly-crosslinked, reprocessable vitrimers through incomplete depolymerization using glycerol as a cleaving agent.

Abstract
--------

Polyethylene terephthalate (PET) is a plastic material commonly applied to beverage packaging used in everyday life. Owing to PET’s versatility and ease of use, its consumption has continuously increased, resulting in considerable waste generation. Several physical and chemical recycling processes have been developed to address this problem. Recently, biological upcycling is being actively studied and has come to be regarded as a powerful technology for overcoming the economic issues associated with conventional recycling methods. For upcycling, PET should be degraded into small molecules, such as terephthalic acid and ethylene glycol, which are utilized as substrates for bioconversion, through various degradation processes, including gasification, pyrolysis, and chemical/biological depolymerization. Furthermore, biological upcycling methods have been applied to biosynthesize value-added chemicals, such as adipic acid, muconic acid, catechol, vanillin, and glycolic acid. In this review, we introduce and discuss various degradation methods that yield substrates for bioconversion and biological upcycling processes to produce value-added biochemicals. These technologies encourage a circular economy, which reduces the amount of waste released into the environment.

**Keywords:** Polyethylene terephthalate (PET), substrate production for bioconversion, biological upcycling, value-added chemicals

Introduction
------------

Plastics are ubiquitous in daily life as they are used in packaging, construction, and clothing [[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9895998/#ref1)]. These materials are significant in modern society owing to their adaptability, low manufacturing costs, and desirable physical features, which include flexibility, lightness, impermeability, and durability [[2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9895998/#ref2), [3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9895998/#ref3)]. Consequently, the demand for plastics has continuously increased; however, improper recycling of plastic waste causes plastic pollution [[4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9895998/#ref4)]. Plastic accumulation threatens human survival and the environment [[5](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9895998/#ref5)]. According to Geyer _et al_. [[6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9895998/#ref6)], as of 2015, 6,300 million metric tons of plastic waste have been disposed of worldwide, and this amount is increasing by 3%annually. Of all the entire plastic waste generated, only 9% is recycled, 79% is landfilled or discarded into oceans, and 12% is incinerated [[7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9895998/#ref7)]. Owing to the low rate of recycling and natural disintegration, plastic usage has caused various environmental issues [[8](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9895998/#ref8)], such as microplastic creation in marine and terrestrial ecologies and plastic island evolution [[9](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9895998/#ref9)]. Approximately 5-13 million tons of plastic are estimated to end up in the ocean each year, and 5 trillion plastic particles, which are fatal to aquatic life, now float in the world’s oceans [[2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9895998/#ref2), [10](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9895998/#ref10)]. Plastic waste is primarily downcycled into less recyclable and cheap products like garden furniture and plant pots [[7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9895998/#ref7)]. Currently, with the changing living conditions attributed to the COVID-19 pandemic, plastic production and pollution have been on the increase, and with the low recycling rate, the effects of this pollution continue to worsen and further endanger the environment. Therefore, such man-made disasters must be rapidly mitigated.

Reuse, incineration, and landfilling are conventional methods for managing plastic waste [[9](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9895998/#ref9)]. Plastic incineration provides energy but also emits particulate matter and toxic gases such as unburned hydrocarbons, nitrous and sulfurous oxides, furans, and dioxins, which cause severe environmental damage [[11](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9895998/#ref11)] For these reasons, landfilling and incineration are not recommended for plastic garbage disposal, and instead, more sustainable and environmentally friendly methods are required [[12](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9895998/#ref12)].

Due to its exceptional mechanical properties, thermal stability, and impermeability to gases and liquids, polyethylene terephthalate (PET) is the most popular synthetic plastic material applied in beverage packaging, food containers, bottles, and textiles [[13](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9895998/#ref13)]. PET is produced by polycondensation of terephthalic acid (TPA) and ethylene glycol (EG) or trans-esterification of dimethyl terephthalate (DMT) and EG. Its stability and resistance to hydrolytic or enzymatic degradation have led to PET being the most commonly found plastic waste in the environment [[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9895998/#ref1)]. Currently, PET is primarily recycled via physical and chemical methods; various studies have also reported the use of biodegradation using microorganisms to break down PET into its monomers: TPA and EG. Physical recycling involves heat treatment at high temperatures, and PET is degraded into downcycled products because of the loss of mechanical properties, which are different from those of virgin PET [[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9895998/#ref1)]. Chemical recycling uses chemicals and expensive catalysts to decompose waste into monomers; recycled plastics are typically more expensive than virgin plastics and are not economically viable [[13](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9895998/#ref13)]. To solve the plastic pollution problem, various approaches, including biological depolymerization and upcycling of plastic wastes, and even plastic-eating microbes, have been considered [[14](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9895998/#ref14)]. Circular repurposing of plastic materials is critical for building a socioeconomic ecosystem without contributing to plastic waste [[15](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9895998/#ref15)]. Upcycling converts waste materials into products of greater worth and quality in their second life [[16](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9895998/#ref16)]. Upcycling also helps achieve the circular economic feasibility of plastic waste with complete recyclability and no loss of value or usability, in contrast to other recycling methods.

In this review, we discuss the development of the upcycling processes suitable for PET, comprising two steps: 1) substrate production from PET waste, and 2) upcycling of the substrate into value-added chemicals ([Fig. 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9895998/#F1)). For producing substrates by degrading PET, we suggest processes suitable for bioconversion to obtain value-added chemicals after reviewing various existing processes, including pyrolysis, gasification, and depolymerization, along with their advantages and disadvantages. Following the review of chemical and biological upcycling models, we propose an efficient upcycling model for producing value-added chemicals. Finally, we suggest a method for PET upcycling to achieve economic feasibility and a circular economy regarding the plastic life cycle, as a means to address plastic environmental pollution.

Production of Substrates from PET Waste for Bioconversion Processes
-------------------------------------------------------------------

Various processes, including pyrolysis, gasification, and depolymerization, can be applied to produce substrates from PET waste ([Fig. 2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9895998/#F2), [Table 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9895998/#T1)). Although individual processes for substrate production from PET waste are well established, selecting a suitable upcycling process, particularly regarding biological upcycling, is crucial because of the economic feasibility and establishment of refineries. Therefore, in this section, we discuss the status of research, along with the advantages and disadvantages, to suggest a process suitable for upcycling.

Substrate Production by Pyrolysis of PET
----------------------------------------

Pyrolysis is the thermal transformation of polymers into liquid fuels and value-added products at considerably high temperatures (300-900°C) in the absence of oxygen [[17](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9895998/#ref17)-[19](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9895998/#ref19)]. PET pyrolysis releases products such as TPA, vinyl terephthalate, aromatic compounds (namely toluene and benzene); esters such as vinyl benzoate; carboxylic acids such as ethyl and methyl benzoic acid; and aliphatic hydrocarbons such as ethane and methanol. TPA formed during pyrolysis can be used for upcycling after purification; however, TPA clogs the equipment [[20](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9895998/#ref20)]. Pyrolysis is affected by factors like substrate type and time, and temperature, which influence the polymer degradation rate; and catalyst loading, which improves the process efficiency through time reduction. Slow, fast, and flash pyrolysis methods are based on reaction times, and microwave pyrolysis is based on the apparatus type [[21](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9895998/#ref21)].

PET waste was pyrolyzed for energy recovery at 500°C, yielding solids, gas, and residue products of 24.33, 35.67, and 74.67 wt%, respectively [[22](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9895998/#ref22)]. The effect of plastic waste loading was studied using microwave co-pyrolysis of PET and rice husk mixtures at 600°C for 4-13 min, which yielded syngas (29%) affected by increasing plastic waste loading, biocrude, and char [[23](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9895998/#ref23)]. Slow pyrolysis of PET waste was conducted at 400°C producing gas, solid residue, and oil at 13.3 ± 0.6, 39.7 ± 1.6, and 46.7 ± 1.9 wt%, respectively [[24](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9895998/#ref24)]. Catalytic pyrolysis of PET was performed to study the effect of temperature and catalyst on PET degradation (500°C in a fixed-bed, semi-batch reactor using a Lewis-Brønsted acid side catalyst). The increase in temperature increased the PET conversion rate, forming liquid-, gas-, and solid-phase products with benzoic acid and TPA. This process is primarily affected by the formation of char, which influences the conversion rate and product yield [[25](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9895998/#ref25)]. Pyrolysis does not require intense waste sorting and feedstock pre-treatment, and is consequently convenient and flexible; hence, it is economical and less labor-intensive. Moreover, pyrolysis reduces the dependence on conventional energy sources (such as fossil fuels), the volume of PET waste, and the carbon footprint of plastic products by reducing carbon monoxide and carbon dioxide emissions [[26](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9895998/#ref26)]. However, pyrolysis produces detrimental materials such as biphenyls, which create environmental pollution and health problems [[27](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9895998/#ref27)]. The substrates required for biological upcycling in this process are expensive as they require purification.

Substrate Production by PET Gasification
----------------------------------------

Gasification is the degradation of plastics at high temperatures (700-1300°C) using oxidizing agents such as oxygen, steam, and air to produce syngas or producer gas consisting of methane, carbon monoxide, and hydrogen gas [[28](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9895998/#ref28)]. Depending on the gasifying agent and heterogeneous char gasification reactions, gasification involves drying, devolatilization, tar cracking, combustion, and shifting [[28](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9895998/#ref28)]. This process is mainly affected by factors including the type of plastic (aliphatic or aromatic), bed material, temperature, and gasifying agent [[29](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9895998/#ref29)]. Depending on the oxidizing agent, steam gasification, air gasification, and co-gasification, wherein different compounds are mixed before gasification, have been explored. Dolomite is used in the fluidized bed to reduce agglomeration and coke formation on active carbon. Active carbon is predominantly used as a tar remover, improving syngas quality [[30](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9895998/#ref30)]. Steam gasification uses steam as the primary hydrogen source. Considering the effect of the operating conditions on the tar and gas composition during the steam gasification of PET, increasing the temperature improves the hydrogen and carbon monoxide yields as the residence time and steam-to-fuel ratio are increased; and carbon monoxide and carbon dioxide contents are decreased [[28](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9895998/#ref28)]. Air gasification of PET was performed in a two-stage gasifier with active carbon. Air was used instead of steam to provide hydrogen and carbon monoxide, yielding producer gas (90.93 wt%), char (6.15%), tar (2.90%), and condensate liquid (0.03 wt%)[[29](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9895998/#ref29)]. Gasification is considerably flexible in treating various feedstock composites and can be integrated into current energy and fuel production systems. However, PET exhibits low effective carbon conversion, which reduces syngas heat. During steam gasification, less than 30% of carbon in PET is converted into gaseous products at 700°C. This conversion is required to form CO 2, which is an important constituent of syngas; however, PET produces 3-4 times less carbon dioxide [[28](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9895998/#ref28)].

Substrate Production via Plastic Depolymerization
-------------------------------------------------

depolymerization is the application of polymer chemistry to undo polymerization reactions to yield PET monomers, _e.g._, bis(2-hydroxyethyl) terephthalate (BHET) and mono(2-hydroxyethyl) terephthalate (MHET) by glycolysis, a typical chemical depolymerization process. The product portfolio can be altered according to the depolymerization method used, which may include chemical, biological, or chemoenzymatic processes. Therefore, in this section, we review the recently developed status and product portfolios along with the advantages and disadvantages of individual depolymerization to select a suitable process for upcycling.

Chemical Depolymerization
-------------------------

Chemical depolymerization uses chemicals and catalysts to influence the breakdown of PET into its monomers, BHET, MHET, TPA, and EG obtained from glycolysis, DMT from methanolysis, and TPA derivatives from aminolysis. Chemical depolymerization methods include alcoholysis (methanolysis), aminolysis, hydrolysis (steam, mineral acids, water, and alkalis), and glycolysis [[31](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9895998/#ref31)]. Inorganic catalysts, organocatalysts, and ionic liquids have been used to accelerate depolymerization.

Aminolysis involves the reaction of PET in primary amine-rich solutions, namely, hydrazine, ethanolamine, methylamine, allylamine, and ethylamine, to produce TPA and EG diamides. PET is used in the form of powder or fibers (temperature range = 20-100°C) [[32](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9895998/#ref32)]. Waste PET bottles were converted into hydrogel adsorbents through aminolysis. PET was reacted with tri- and tetraamines, diethylene amine, and diethylene-tetraamine, producing monomers, dimers, and oligomers, respectively, which were interlinked with ethylene glycol diglycidyl ether to form hydrogels [[33](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9895998/#ref33)]. Additionally, using an ultrafast microwave, aminolysis of PET could be conducted without catalysts in the primary amine-rich solutions at 180-200°C. Highlights
----------

*   •This comprehensive review summarizes bio-upcycling technologies for major plastics. 
*   •Synthetic biology and depolymerization technologies enable plastic upcycling. 
*   •Discusses advances in tandem chemical and biological plastic upcycling. 
*   •Presents ways to overcome challenges that hinder advances in plastic upcycling. 

Abstract
--------

Each year, millions of tons of plastics are produced for use in such applications as packaging, construction, and textiles. While plastic is undeniably useful and convenient, its environmental fate and transport have raised growing concerns about waste and pollution. However, the ease and low cost of producing virgin plastic have so far made conventional plastic recycling economically unattractive. Common contaminants in plastic waste and shortcomings of the recycling processes themselves typically mean that recycled plastic products are of relatively low quality in some cases. The high cost and high energy requirements of typical recycling operations also reduce their economic benefits. In recent years, the bio-upcycling of chemically treated plastic waste has emerged as a promising alternative to conventional plastic recycling. Unlike recycling, bio-upcycling uses relatively mild process conditions to economically transform pretreated plastic waste into value-added products. In this review, we first provide a précis of the general methodology and limits of conventional plastic recycling. Then, we review recent advances in hybrid chemical/biological upcycling methods for different plastics, including polyethylene terephthalate, polyurethane, polyamide, polycarbonate, polyethylene, polypropylene, polystyrene, and polyvinyl chloride. For each kind of plastic, we summarize both the pretreatment methods for making the plastic bio-available and the microbial chassis for degrading or converting the treated plastic waste to value-added products. We also discuss both the limitations of upcycling processes for major plastics and their potential for bio-upcycling.

1. Introduction
---------------

Plastic is ubiquitous in our daily lives. In a continuous upward trend, over 400 million metric tons of plastic were produced globally in 2022 ([PlasticsEurope, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0734975024001563#bb0505)). In decreasing order of weight, the top six most produced plastics were polyethylene (PE), polypropylene (PP), polyvinyl chloride (PVC), polyethylene terephthalate (PET), polyurethane (PUR), and polystyrene (PS) ([PlasticsEurope, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0734975024001563#bb0505)). Plastics play vital roles in packaging, building construction, the automotive industry, the electrical and electronic industry, and the medical field ([Porta et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0734975024001563#bb0515)). However, less than 10% (35.6 million metric tons) of the globally produced plastics came from recycled or bio-based sources in 2022, while most produced plastics are made from chemicals derived from fossil ([PlasticsEurope, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0734975024001563#bb0505)). Worsening the problem, almost 80% of produced plastics end up as waste in landfills or oceans because of the high production and low recycling of plastics. However, even under laboratory conditions, detailed reports exist only for PET's bio-assimilation, while other plastics have only limited reports, especially non-hydrolyzable plastics like PE, PP, and PVC, proving the difficulties of natural plastic biodegradation ([Wei et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0734975024001563#bb0730)). The resulting accumulation of plastic waste has caused serious environmental problems and health issues for both animals and humans.

Plastic waste not only occupies a large amount of space in landfills but also produces microplastics from degradation and weathering. These microplastics facilitate the plastic additive leachability and the mobility of plastic wastes and substances absorbed by microplastics, such as heavy metals and antibiotics ([Kumar et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0734975024001563#bb0345)). The accumulation of microplastics in the soil can alter the soil's properties and structure over time, which affects the activities and diversities of microbial populations and plants ([Wojnowska-Baryla et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0734975024001563#bb0755)). Plastic pollution also enters the ocean at an alarming rate; each year, 7.8 million metric tons of plastic carbon settle on the seafloor and affect more than 914 marine megafaunal species ([Kuhn and van Franeker, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0734975024001563#bb0340); [Smeaton, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0734975024001563#bb0630)). Furthermore, microplastics ingested by animals ultimately pass up the food chain to humans and have been associated with DNA damage, cytotoxicity, inflammation, respiratory stress, autoimmune disease, cardiovascular diseases, bowel diseases, and various cancers in various studies ([Kumar et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0734975024001563#bb0345); [Marfella et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0734975024001563#bib829), [Yan et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0734975024001563#bib830)).

Traditionally, plastic recycling is classified into four levels ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0734975024001563#f0005)). The first level, primary recycling, is the mechanical recycling, via melting and extrusion, of pure pre-consumer plastic waste from the plastic manufacturing process ([Schyns and Shaver, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0734975024001563#bb0595)). After primary recycling, these materials can be reintroduced into the manufacturing process. Secondary recycling is the mechanical recycling of sorted processed post-consumer plastic ([Larrain et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0734975024001563#bb0350)). Tertiary recycling is the chemical recycling process of such plastic waste. Chemical recycling regenerates monomers and other chemicals from plastic wastes for virgin plastic production or other purposes ([Chen et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0734975024001563#bb0110)). Quaternary recycling is the recovery of energy via burning or pyrolysis products from plastic wastes that are not suitable for mechanical or chemical recycling due to low quality, processing complexity, and operation cost. Pyrolysis can also generate some chemicals as by-products ([Schyns and Shaver, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0734975024001563#bb0595)). Except for quaternary recycling, these recycling processes are close-looped and aim to regenerate the original plastic material. Thus, their products directly compete with virgin plastics from petroleum products in both quality and price. However, due to the current relatively low price of petroleum, the plastic recycling industry is an inherently low-profit business.

To solve this dilemma, the concept of plastic upcycling has been gaining interest in recent years. An upcycling process transforms useless or low-value materials into new materials with new functions and purposes. Instead of reproducing the virgin plastics, upcycling generates higher-value products from waste plastics. Upcycling of several kinds of plastic has been achieved in physical and chemical approaches, including hydrogenolysis, oxidization, carbonization, or a mixed tandem process. When applied to PE and PP, hydrogenolysis can convert them into alkanes and liquid hydrocarbons for application as fuel and lubricants, and when applied to plastics such as PET and polycarbonate (PC) that contain aromatic groups, it can generate various aromatic compounds ([Wei et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0734975024001563#bb0740)). Moreover, PE, PP, PS, and PVC can be oxidized into high-value organic acids and aromatic compounds under certain conditions ([Zhang et al., 2022c](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0734975024001563#bb0815)). Carbonization produces carbon nanomaterials, such as nanoporous carbon and carbon nanotubes, which play essential roles in supercapacitors and lithium-ion batteries ([Zhang et al., 2022c](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0734975024001563#bb0815)).

Although some newly developed methods generally require less intensive or simpler reaction conditions compared to certain chemical recycling processes and pyrolysis, there are still many challenges presenting in these processes ([Zhang et al., 2022c](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0734975024001563#bb0815)). For example, hydrogenolysis and carbonization require high temperatures and produce limited types of products. Oxidation and functionalization of polymers are still not selective enough and happen randomly along the polymer backbone. Finally, these reactions usually require complex and expensive catalysts that must be meticulously designed. Thus, researchers have already started to look for alternative technologies. In recent years, plastic-degrading microbes have been isolated from soil and water exposed to plastic wastes, such as landfills ([Gambarini et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0734975024001563#bb0185)). As a result, strains and genes have been identified and have shown great potential for upcycling plastics into value-added products through biotransformation. Although the natural degradation of plastics by microorganisms is quite slow, researchers have found a workaround by combining the fast chemical degradation of plastic with microbes' metabolic potential to utilize chemically degraded products as their feedstocks, thereby enabling a fast and environmental-friendly way to valorize plastic waste.

This review discusses research advances in tandem chemical-biological plastic upcycling ([Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0734975024001563#t0010)). The application of synthetic biology tools to an array of microorganisms has yielded multiple promising biorefinery platforms for plastic wastes. These studies highlight synthetic biology's ability to shape a sustainable and profitable future for plastic waste management. This review also provides a current view of tandem chemical-biological plastic upcycling. The multifaceted nature of plastic waste means there is no one-size-fits-all solution, and each type of plastic presents unique challenges. This review also presents the different techniques and strategies employed by researchers to date to overcome these challenges and identifies the specific challenges hindering technical advances in upcycling a particular type of plastic.

2. Overview of conventional plastic recycling
---------------------------------------------

Currently, the most commercially applied plastic recycling process is mechanical recycling. Both primary and secondary recycling rely on the mechanical recycling method, and the critical difference is the origin of the plastic waste, as mentioned ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0734975024001563#f0005)). A complete secondary recycling process typically involves the following sequence of steps: shredding, washing, a first milling, float/sink separation, drying, wind sifting, a second milling, and, finally, melting and extrusion ([Larrain et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0734975024001563#bb0350)). The mixed plastic waste is first shredded for easier cleaning to remove contaminants from usage and environmental exposure. After being washed, the shredded plastic enters the first milling process to reduce its sizes for further float/sink separation, which can separate different types of plastics based on their densities ([Bauer et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0734975024001563#bb0070)). The separated plastics are then sorted into different streams for drying. The second milling process then reduces the dried plastics into different sizes for the following extrusion process.

Extrusion, the core process in mechanical recycling, is applicable on a large scale for multiple plastic types and is relatively cheap to perform. Plastic waste from the second milling will be fed into an extruder, with heated rotating screws softening and pushing plastics toward the die to form extrudate. However, the heat and shear force during extrusion could degrade the plastic's mechanical properties and further processability. The heating and shear forces promote the oxidization of the plastics, which generate radicals that cause chain scissions and shorten chain lengths ([Gryn'ova et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0734975024001563#bb0215)). Depending on the type and quality of the plastic wastes and mechanical recycling conditions, recycled plastics can maintain their performance over several cycles of processing, but the plastic quality will inevitably degrade due to contaminations and repeated heating processes ([Schyns and Shaver, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0734975024001563#bb0595)).

Once a plastic's quality is below the requirements for mechanical recycling, chemical recycling can be employed. For hydrolyzable plastics such as PET, PU, and polyamide, chemical recycling breaks plastic wastes down into corresponding monomers, which are then used to produce new virgin plastics; for non-hydrolyzable plastics like PP and PE, it is impossible to recover the monomers used for production with current technologies ([Wei et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0734975024001563#bb0730)). However, only 0.1 million metric tons of plastic waste are being chemically recycled each year ([PlasticsEurope, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0734975024001563#bb0505)). An ideal chemical recycling process is selective and specific for a type of plastic, where the monomers produced can be easily separated and reused while maintaining purity. However, the selectivity of chemical reactions will greatly increase the research and operational costs for each separate production line. Non-selective chemical processes such as pyrolysis and hydrocracking usually generate mixed compounds and increase operating expenses due to the complexity of recovering individual products ([Zhang et al., 2022c](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0734975024001563#bb0815)). Although chemical recycling can regain more value from plastic waste, the high costs of operation and separation processes often offset the values of plastic monomers. enriched_points.point_title: Overview of mechanical, chemical, biological, and hybrid methods
enriched_points.expanded_content: Mechanical methods involve shredding and remelting plastics, while chemical methods use solvents and catalysts to break down polymers. Biological techniques utilize microorganisms for degradation, and hybrid methods combine both approaches for efficient upcycling.

enriched_points.point_title: Advantages and limitations of each technique
enriched_points.expanded_content: Mechanical recycling is energy-efficient but may degrade material quality. Chemical methods can regenerate monomers but involve high costs. Biological methods are eco-friendly, yet slower. Hybrid techniques optimize both efficiency and sustainability.

Choosing the right upcycling method is crucial for maximizing resource recovery, minimizing environmental impact, and ensuring economic feasibility in transforming waste plastics into valuable products.

Emerging trends focus on integrating advanced technologies like synthetic biology and machine learning to enhance upcycling efficiency, reduce costs, and develop more sustainable materials from plastic waste.
